front: Chapter 1 — Introduction. What is **arithmetic progression**?
back: **arithmetic progression** a sequence of numbers where the difference between any two consecutive numbers is constant.

front: Chapter 1 — Introduction. What is **geometric progression**?
back: **geometric progression** a sequence of numbers where the ratio between any two consecutive numbers is constant.

front: Chapter 1 — Introduction. What is **set**?
back: **set** a collection of elements.

front: Chapter 1 — Introduction. What is **predicate**?
back: **predicate** an expression that is true or false depending on its parameters.

front: Chapter 2 — Time complexity. What is **square root algorithm**?
back: **square root algorithm** slower than $O$(log$n$) but faster than $O(n)$.

front: Chapter 3 — Sorting. What is **Sorting**?
back: **Sorting** a fundamental algorithm design problem.

front: Chapter 3 — Sorting. What is **comparison operator**?
back: **comparison operator** defined for the data type of the elements to be sorted.

front: Chapter 4 — Data structures. What is **data structure**?
back: **data structure** a way to store data in the memory of a computer.

front: Chapter 4 — Data structures. What is **dynamic array**?
back: **dynamic array** an array whose size can be changed during the execution of the program.

front: Chapter 4 — Data structures. What is **set**?
back: **set** a data structure that maintains a collection of elements.

front: Chapter 4 — Data structures. What is **map**?
back: **map** a generalized array that consists of key-value-pairs.

front: Chapter 4 — Data structures. What is **iterator**?
back: **iterator** a variable that points to an element in a data structure.

front: Chapter 4 — Data structures. What is **bitset**?
back: **bitset** an array whose each value is either 0 or 1.

front: Chapter 4 — Data structures. What is **deque**?
back: **deque** a dynamic array whose size can be efficiently changed at both ends of the array.

front: Chapter 4 — Data structures. What is **stack**?
back: **stack** a data structure that provides two $O$(1) time operations: adding an element to the top, and removing an element from the top.

front: Chapter 5 — Complete search. What is **Complete search**?
back: **Complete search** a general method that can be used to solve almost any algorithm problem.

front: Chapter 5 — Complete search. What is **Meet in the middle**?
back: **Meet in the middle** a technique where the search space is divided into two parts of about equal size.

front: Chapter 7 — Dynamic programming. What is **Dynamic programming**?
back: **Dynamic programming** a technique that combines the correctness of complete search and the efficiency of greedy algorithms.

front: Chapter 7 — Dynamic programming. What is **knapsack**?
back: **knapsack** problems where a set of objects is given, and subsets with some properties have to be found.

front: Chapter 8 — Amortized analysis. What is **sliding window**?
back: **sliding window** a constant-size subarray that moves from left to right through the array.

front: Chapter 10 — Bit manipulation. What is **Two’s complement**?
back: **Two’s complement** used, which means that the opposite number of a number is calculated by first inverting all the bits in the number, and then increasing the number by one.

front: Chapter 11 — Basics of graphs. What is **tree**?
back: **tree** a connected graph that consists of $n$ nodes and $n-1$ edges.

front: Chapter 11 — Basics of graphs. What is **adjacency matrix**?
back: **adjacency matrix** a two-dimensional array that indicates which edges the graph contains.

front: Chapter 14 — Tree algorithms. What is **tree**?
back: **tree** a connected, acyclic graph that consists of $n$ nodes and $n -1$ edges.

front: Chapter 14 — Tree algorithms. What is **binary tree**?
back: **binary tree** a rooted tree where each node has a left and right subtree.

front: Chapter 15 — Spanning trees. What is **minimum spanning tree**?
back: **minimum spanning tree** a spanning tree whose weight is as small as possible.

front: Chapter 15 — Spanning trees. What is **maximum spanning tree**?
back: **maximum spanning tree** a spanning tree whose weight is as large as possible.

front: Chapter 16 — Directed graphs. What is **topological sort**?
back: **topological sort** an ordering of the nodes of a directed graph such that if there is a path from node $a$ to node $b$, then node $a$ appears before node $b$ in the ordering.

front: Chapter 19 — Paths and circuits. What is **Eulerian path**?
back: **Eulerian path** a path that goes through each edge exactly once.

front: Chapter 19 — Paths and circuits. What is **Hamiltonian path**?
back: **Hamiltonian path** a path that visits each node exactly once.

front: Chapter 19 — Paths and circuits. What is **Eulerian circuit**?
back: **Eulerian circuit** an Eulerian path that starts and ends at the same node.

front: Chapter 19 — Paths and circuits. What is **De Bruijn sequence**?
back: **De Bruijn sequence** a string that contains every string of length $n$ exactly once as a substring, for a fixed alphabet of $k$ characters.

front: Chapter 19 — Paths and circuits. What is **knight’s tour**?
back: **knight’s tour** a sequence of moves of a knight on an $n \times n$ chessboard following the rules of chess such that the knight visits each square exactly once.

front: Chapter 19 — Paths and circuits. What is **Warnsdorf’s rule**?
back: **Warnsdorf’s rule** a simple and effective heuristic for finding a knight’s tour$^3$.

front: Chapter 20 — Flows and cuts. What is **path cover**?
back: **path cover** a set of paths in a graph such that each node of the graph belongs to at least one path.

front: Chapter 20 — Flows and cuts. What is **general path cover**?
back: **general path cover** a path cover where a node can belong to more than one path.

front: Chapter 20 — Flows and cuts. What is **antichain**?
back: **antichain** a set of nodes of a graph such that there is no path from any node to another node using the edges of the graph.

front: Chapter 21 — Number theory. What is **Number theory**?
back: **Number theory** a branch of mathematics that studies integers.

front: Chapter 21 — Number theory. What is **sieve of Eratosthenes**?
back: **sieve of Eratosthenes** a preprocessing algorithm that builds an array using which we can efficiently check if a given number between 2.

front: Chapter 21 — Number theory. What is **Pythagorean triple**?
back: **Pythagorean triple** a triple $(a, b, c)$ that satisfies the Pythagorean theorem $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$, which means that there is a right triangle with side lengths $a$, $b$ and $c$.

front: Chapter 22 — Combinatorics. What is **Inclusion-exclusion**?
back: **Inclusion-exclusion** a technique that can be used for counting the size of a union of sets when the sizes of the intersections are known, and vice versa.

front: Chapter 22 — Combinatorics. What is **Prüfer code**?
back: **Prüfer code** a sequence of $n-2$ numbers that describes a labeled tree.

front: Chapter 23 — Matrices. What is **matrix**?
back: **matrix** a mathematical concept that corresponds to a two-dimensional array in programming.

front: Chapter 23 — Matrices. What is **identity matrix**?
back: **identity matrix** a square matrix where each element on the diagonal is 1 and all other elements are 0.

front: Chapter 24 — Probability. What is **probability**?
back: **probability** a real number between 0 and 1 that indicates how probable an event is.

front: Chapter 24 — Probability. What is **random variable**?
back: **random variable** a value that is generated by a random process.

front: Chapter 24 — Probability. What is **Markov chain**?
back: **Markov chain** a random process that consists of states and transitions between them.

front: Chapter 24 — Probability. What is **randomized algorithm**?
back: **randomized algorithm** an algorithm that is based on randomness.

front: Chapter 24 — Probability. What is **Monte Carlo algorithm**?
back: **Monte Carlo algorithm** a randomized algorithm that may sometimes give a wrong answer.

front: Chapter 24 — Probability. What is **Las Vegas algorithm**?
back: **Las Vegas algorithm** a randomized algorithm that always gives the correct answer, but its running time varies randomly.

front: Chapter 25 — Game theory. What is **winning state**?
back: **winning state** a state where the player will win the game if they play optimally, and a **losing state** is a state where the player will lose the game if the opponent plays optimally.

front: Chapter 25 — Game theory. What is **nim game**?
back: **nim game** a simple game that has an important role in game theory, because many other games can be played using the same strategy.

front: Chapter 26 — String algorithms. What is **substring**?
back: **substring** a sequence of consecutive characters in a string.

front: Chapter 26 — String algorithms. What is **subsequence**?
back: **subsequence** a sequence of (not necessarily consecutive) characters in a string in their original order.

front: Chapter 26 — String algorithms. What is **prefix**?
back: **prefix** a substring that starts at the beginning of a string, and a **suffix** is a substring that ends at the end of a string.

front: Chapter 26 — String algorithms. What is **period**?
back: **period** a prefix of a string such that the string can be constructed by repeating the period.

front: Chapter 26 — String algorithms. What is **border**?
back: **border** a string that is both a prefix and a suffix of a string.

front: Chapter 26 — String algorithms. What is **trie**?
back: **trie** a rooted tree that maintains a set of strings.

front: Chapter 26 — String algorithms. What is **String hashing**?
back: **String hashing** a technique that allows us to efficiently check whether two strings are equal$^1$.

front: Chapter 27 — Square root algorithms. What is **square root algorithm**?
back: **square root algorithm** an algorithm that has a square root in its time complexity.

front: Chapter 30 — Sweep line algorithms. What is **convex hull**?
back: **convex hull** the smallest convex polygon that contains all points of a given set.
